title: The Secret YouTube Strategy Dominating in 2026

transcript.text: There's a strategy YouTubers are using that is blowing up their channels and their audiences don't even realize it's happening and I'm going to tell you all about it, but first a quick history lesson. You've probably heard this tale from the Trojan War. The Greeks have been camped outside of Troy for 10 years and could not get in. So eventually they stopped trying to break down the door and instead built a giant horse. They left it on the beach and then waited to get invited in and this turned the tide of the war. And believe it or not that same strategy to a degree is being employed all across YouTube with channels pulling in massive amounts of views from topics that on the surface will be kind of boring. May I interest you in a think piece on the over optimization of engagement? No? Okay, fine. How about a video on the death of Tinder? That's what this channel did with only a thousand subscribers and it got over 300,000 views. Would you be interested in clicking on a video about statistical sampling methodologies? Oh, wait, no, I'm sorry. I meant how many trees are there in Skyrim? Neither of these topics are trending and in fact the content within is not something the average person would just search for on a random Tuesday. But these creators figured out the same thing the Greeks did 3,000 years ago. The Trojan horse wasn't just a horse, it was a gift. It had the right shape. It was made of the right material and it kind of looked like something the Trojans already wanted. What they didn't know is that inside was, you know, a whole bunch of soldiers ready to jump out and kill them. What you put on the outside determines if anybody picks it up. What you put on the inside is what you want to deliver and ideally the thing you want to deliver doesn't kill anybody. You might think that I'm saying this is the title and the thumbnail, but I'm not. It's actually the lens your entire video is made through and by the end of this video you'll understand how to use it for your channel. Let's start with some channels you've already heard of and then we'll move on to some channels that are a lot more up and coming. Take Mark Rober. You may have seen his squirrel ninja warrior obstacle course from a while back. On the surface it's just chaos. Squirrels flying, tumblers spinning, and photo ops taken just to get some walnuts in a bird feeder. But underneath that it's engineering and physics and behavioral science. You certainly didn't click for that, but you watched it, you enjoyed it, you learned something, you stuck around. That one video pulled in over 144 million views, and it's not because that engineering principles are some kind of trending topic. It's because a former NASA employee got you to root for a squirrel. Next, we'll take a look at Cleo Abram who runs Huge If True. She talks about quantum computers, supersonic planes, the Large Hadron Collider, stuff most people would scroll straight past. Except her channel has 7.5 million subscribers and 2.2 billion views. She never opens the video with the topic at hand. Instead, she starts by talking about the thing you already have feelings about. For example, her F1 video. On the surface, it's a beginner's guide to a sport everyone's suddenly obsessed with, but inside the video is a real argument about whether extreme expensive R&D is ever actually justified. >> crazy expensive sport that everyone's suddenly obsessed with. >> LEWIS HAMILTON! >> VERSTAPPEN HOLDS IT! >> HE'S DONE! >> BUT let's also look at the bigger question here, the huge question, which is why do this? Like, why pour so much money? >> Grant Sanderson does the same exact thing with math. His channel 3Blue1Brown covers neural networks, linear algebra, the Riemann hypothesis, and yet 8 million subscribers. Which video would you click on? Option A, how distributed ledger verification works, or option B, how does Bitcoin actually work? It's the same video. Option A is what's inside the box, option B is what goes on the outside. But here's where it gets interesting. Big channels, this makes a lot of sense, right? They have teams and budgets, and they've built years of trust. People click because of who made the video. Small channels don't have that luxury, though. They can't rely on loyalty. When a channel with 10,000 subscribers suddenly pulls 600,000 views on a new video, something else is going on here. And that something else is this. Sierra LaDuke is a new creator who just had her first video blow up, getting 380,000 views and 9,000 subscribers in under a month. On the surface, it's a commentary on work culture, lazy girl jobs, quiet quitting, very clickable stuff in 2026. But the actual video is a philosophical argument on tracing the history of human labor from medieval craftsmen to the creator economy. Making the case that for the first time in 200 years, ordinary people are getting their tools back. >> What spellbinds me as an idea is that I'll be able to sit someplace in a railroad station and write a movie. >> This was the dream in the 1960s for tech. >> She packaged her video on a feeling that millions of people have every single day. And once inside, she pulled a bait and switch that nobody saw coming. >> I'm standing now because I wanted to stretch my legs and not because I'm filming this part of the video on a completely different day for no reason at all. Honestly, this example is just too good not to include in this video after the fact, but have you ever opened up your favorite snack, taken a bite only to realize they've changed the recipe on you? It's certainly happened to me. It's even happened to my cat. And that sucks because cats don't understand English and they don't really get why their favorite food now tastes like trash. Pets aside, this is a common grievance on the Girl Gone London channel and the Trojan horse that she uses in her content. Her breakout video was technically about corporate acquisition strategy, Kraft's hostile takeover of Cadbury in 2010. It featured broken promises to British workers, supply chain optimization, pretty much topics that are relatively dry for the YouTube crowd. But the title of the video is you're not going crazy, why Cadbury chocolate tastes different now. You're not clicking because of some acquisition story being pitched to you. Although, that would be interesting to learn, you're clicking because somebody finally said what you've been thinking for a while. And once you're in the door, you get the full picture. The gutting factories, the Bournville model village George Cadbury built for his workers in 1893, stripped all for shareholder value. This video got over 213,000 views on a channel which is primarily about an American living in the UK. The personal grievance is the vehicle for the entire content and the corporate history lesson is what's inside. But now for the most robust version of the strategy that I've seen and it really goes to show how much you can really lean on the Trojan horse strategy on YouTube. Marshall McGee is a professional sound designer with over 121,000 subscribers and only 47 videos, which is kind of wild for a channel that's been around this many years. But when you look at his titles, "Why does Tears of the Kingdom sound so good? Golf used to sound awesome. What happened? Why does Star Wars sound so good?" Every single one of these sounds like a gaming or pop culture video. One you I click on just simply because you love Zelda. Listen a little bit closer to me here a cool detail. "When Link steps with his right foot, only the shield makes noise and when he steps with his left foot, only the arrows and sword make noise." What we're actually watching though is electron tonality, the spectrum between pure sine waves. On the far left we'd have the sine wave, which is just one singular frequency, the most tonal sound that exists. The sine wave is the building block of all sound. My own voice speaking to you right now can be described and broken down as just the sum of thousands of these sine waves at different frequencies and times. He's giving a collegiate-level audio engineering course, but disguising it as a video about Zelda. And here's what separates this channel from the others we've looked at. Sierra Laduke just built one great horse, but Marshall McGee made the horse the entire architecture of his career. He's never once titled a video around the thing he's actually discussing. Instead, he packages for the fandom that he wants to speak to. The whole channel is one continuous Trojan horse and inside is an audio engineering degree. So, what's the underlying pattern here? Well, you need two things. First is the topic that you're going to discuss, the the skill, the idea that you care about and that goes inside the box. Second, what is it that already has an audience? And I don't mean your audience, I mean what has an established audience around it. It can be a sport, a game, a feeling that millions of people carry around inside, a topic that people are already invested in, one they're already clicking on. Your job is to find where those two things genuinely connect. You want to make lo-fi music, but unfortunately, nobody searches for unknown artists. So, you scored every scene from The Office using only lo-fi beats. You want to talk about personal finance, and you happen to love reality TV. So, every real housewife ranked by how fast they'd go broke. Your topic isn't necessarily the problem. You don't want to change what you're trying to deliver. You just need to make the door that people walk through appealing, so they can get to it. But even when you build a great horse, and the packaging is right, and the content is strong, and the value is genuinely there, sometimes your video still doesn't get views. And you might think to yourself, "Well, the algorithm is clearly having a bad day." But sometimes, that's not the case. It's actually the audience's fault. In this video, we talk about the various biases on YouTube that keep videos from getting views sometimes. I'll put it this way. Skipping this video could mean you're missing out on a lot of views. Enjoy.
